narrative: Tata Steel’s FY2025 ESG position was strategically stronger than its operational ranking suggests. It ranked first on 27 indicators and second on 15, but still had 34 rank-four outcomes. Tata remained the scale leader on turnover and the strongest private-sector player on employee gender diversity, while improving wellbeing spend and sustaining 100% training coverage across a very large employee and worker base. Its sustainability investment signal was the strongest in the peer set: 37% sustainability capex and 100% R&D alignment. Tata’s own materiality disclosure acknowledges that blast furnace production is emission-intensive and ties long-term competitiveness to Net Zero 2045, decarbonising BF operations and building a low-emission EAF plant in Ludhiana. Environmentally, the gap remains significant. Tata ranked last on Scope 1, Scope 2, Scope 3 and physical GHG intensity, and its 3.2 tCO2e/tcs was far above Jindal’s 1.85. It also lagged on physical energy intensity, water consumption and several regulated-waste categories. Offsetting this, Tata led waste recovery and materially reduced disposal. Socially, safety remained the biggest weakness: despite ISO 45001-based systems and supplier safety controls, Tata ranked fourth on LTIFR, TRIFR and affected-person metrics. Its vendor assessment model, VCAP programme and reassessment process show governance depth, but FY2025’s message is clear: Tata has the strongest transition architecture, but peers are ahead on disclosed operating outcomes.
